summary: Ronacher argues that most software is built to remember indefinitely, but the deliberate ability to forget is a stronger and more humane design choice. He contrasts digital persistence with the physical world, where objects naturally decay, and that decay provides useful signals about relevance and age. Persistent SaaS artifacts (dashboards, notebooks, docs) become stale, cluttered, and misleading, especially in shared environments. He contends the barrier is not technical difficulty but a mistaken belief that permanence is always a benefit. The conclusion: software should be “ephemeral by default,” with decay, hiding, or scheduled deletion to keep systems useful and current.
key_points: Digital systems lack natural decay, removing useful cues about relevance that exist in the physical world.
Endless persistence in shared tools creates clutter, hesitation to create, and accidental reliance on outdated artifacts.
Visual decay, hiding, and TTL-style lifecycle management are feasible but culturally undervalued in software design.
Ephemeral defaults (garbage collection, staging areas, archives) would reduce clutter while keeping recovery possible.
At minimum, tools should offer scheduled deletion so users can decide relevance at creation time.
money_quotes: Creating software that remembers is easy, but designing software that deliberately “forgets” is a bit more complex.
In the physical world, much of what we create has a natural tendency to decay and that is really useful information.
When data is kept indefinitely by default, it changes our relationship with that software.
I'm not convinced that the work required to implement any of those things is why it does not exist.
I want software to hide old documents, dashboards etc. and that includes most critically not showing up in search.
Ideally software embraced an “ephemeral by default” approach.
Give me a button I can press to schedule deletion.
key_insight: Software should be designed to decay and forget by default because permanence creates clutter, misleads users, and hides the real age and relevance of information.
